experiments.title: How eliminating the highest gift array option from a donation form impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of gift array options for a non donor audience will achieve an increase in conversion because there is reduced friction in the decision making process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2024 - 02/23/2024
When sending non-donor traffic to a donation page after signing a petition, we wondered if having a $200 option in the gift array was necessary or if would harm conversion by making non-donors have more options to consider, and feeling that if they could not make a large gift amount, then they should not give. We wondered if by reducing the friction in the process by taking out the highest option—which we surmised would be the lowest likely option for a non-donor to choose as their first gift—we would see an increase in conversion from this audience. We split traffic 50/50 via email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of gift array options for a non-donor audience led to a significant increase in conversions. By removing the highest option of $200 from the donation page, there was reduced friction in the decision-making process for potential donors, resulting in a 28.5% increase in transactions with 98% level of confidence for the non-donor traffic that qualified for entry in the test. This increase in conversions demonstrates that providing fewer options can help streamline the donation process and encourage more individuals to make a contribution.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of reducing the number of gift array options on conversion rates. This could involve further refining the donation page by eliminating additional high-end donation amounts or simplifying the overall layout to make it easier for potential donors to choose a gift amount. Additionally, it would be valuable to explore how this approach may impact donor retention and overall fundraising success in the long term. By incorporating the learnings from this experiment into future testing and optimization strategies, the Canadian Taxpayers Federation can continue to improve its donation process and drive greater support for its cause.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to the headline for a Dear Reader impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the headline for page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because the new headline is easier and quicker to understand what is being offered to the reader.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2024 - 02/23/2024
When advertising a newsletter on newsroom pages for CTF, we wondered if “A Note for our Readers” was causing banner blindness and not clearly articulating the offer to the page visitors. We set up a treatment that removed the headline and replaced it instead with the subheader which was “Join now to get the Taxpayer newsletter” and split traffic 50/50 between the two versions.
experiments.keyLearnings: By adding clarity in the headline for page visitors, we were able to significantly impact email acquisition rates as we observed a 126% increase in conversion with 99% level of confidence. Replacing a vague headline with a clear and specific message about the offer can lead to a substantial increase in conversions. We believe this increase happened because the new headline "Join now to get the Taxpayer newsletter" effectively communicated the value proposition to the readers, leading to a higher engagement rate. For future experiments, we will look to utilize clear and specific headlines that quickly convey the offer to potential subscribers. We will continue to test different variations of headlines to optimize for the highest email acquisition rates. We will also consider applying similar strategies to other marketing materials to enhance communication effectiveness with the target audience. As we think of future test ideas, we will continue to monitor and analyze the impact of headline changes on key metrics to further refine strategies for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How the principle of scarcity impacted donations from petition signers
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that evoking the principle of scarcity for petition signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because by stating that a limited number of people choose to donate (1%), we are creating a sense of scarcity or exclusivity which can potentially make the opportunity to donate seem more appealing.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2023 - 07/05/2023
When running a petition, we wanted to find out if utilizing the principle of scarcity could motivate more petition signers to make a gift as the final step in the funnel. In the treatment donation page copy, we called their attention to the fact that out of the people who submitted the petition, only 1% would typically step up and actually make a gift to support the organization’s efforts. We ran this against a personal message on a donation page signed by an investigative journalist.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we learned after splitting traffic between the control and the treatment is that the treatment, which called attention to the fact that only 1% of petition signers typically make a donation, resulted in a 63.9% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that creating a sense of scarcity or exclusivity can make the opportunity to donate more appealing to individuals.
The treatment also had a substantial impact on revenue. It led to a 70.3% increase in revenue, indicating that not only did more people donate, but they also gave larger amounts. This result suggests that emphasizing scarcity can not only increase donor conversion but also encourage higher donations from those who do choose to donate.
The scarcity message had a significant impact on new visitors, with a 133.6% increase in donations. This suggests that highlighting scarcity can be particularly effective in capturing the attention and inspiring action from individuals who are interacting with the organization for the first time.
Based on these learnings, future experimentation should focus on further optimizing and targeting the scarcity message. Specifically, testing different variations of scarcity messaging, such as emphasizing limited time offers or exclusive access, can help identify the most effective strategy for driving donor conversion.

experiments.title: How removing lead-in copy from a survey impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in survey submissions because there will be fewer steps to arrive at the beginning of the survey questions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/16/2023
Because most traffic was coming from a Facebook ad that gave the context for why taking the survey was important, we hypothesized that the preamble on the landing page to begin a survey was creating unnecessary friction in the process. We tested removing this section and diving straight into the questions to see how it would impact conversion at the landing page level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we observed after running the experiment was the treatment showed a 58.0% increase in emails acquired with a statistical level of confidence of 99.9% so the results are valid. This experiment suggests that minimizing the number of steps to arrive at the survey questions can improve visitor engagement. The visitors were already consuming the value proposition at the Facebook ad level and did not need further motivation on the landing page to continue the process. We learned that we simply needed to get out of their way and let them begin without the extra step causing friction.

In future experimentation, we will run additional tests that make it simple, quick, and easy for visitors to navigate through the pages. We can start with numbering the questions presented so the user has a better idea of how many questions they will be asked to answer and assess how long it will take them to finish the work involved.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the need for donors to step up for petition signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because visitors will have more clarity and visibility into the needs of the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2023 - 06/25/2023
We were seeing consistently low donor conversion for petition signers and we wondered if calling attention to the low conversion would cause more people to stop and consider helping. Our hypothesis was that if all petition signers assume that CTF is receiving plenty of donations they might feel less compelled to give because they believe that someone else will fill in the gap. We rewrote the donation page to draw the visitor’s attention to the fact that only a small portion of petition signers care enough to take the next step and make an impact on the issue they care about from the petition. In the treatment, we also kept one element from previous testing that shares a testimonial to help persuade visitors to give monthly. We split traffic 50/50 for all petitions.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for two weeks, the treatment resulted in a 54.8% increase in donations with a 100.0% level of confidence. Additionally, CTF received a significant increase in monthly donors on the treatment. We believe the increased callout to ask the donor to demonstrate if they truly believe in the issue they just signed the petition for by asking them to take the next step and make a donation would make them feel like they were taking a more exclusive action than the "average" petition signer. The treatment copy could have made the donors feel more trust in the organization because of the transparency about the status of the needs and low volume of donations, which may have come as surprising information to some visitors if they believed other petition signers were making gifts so there was not a true need for that individual to also make a gift of support. We also believe this increase could be partially due to the shorter length of copy that puts the donation form above the fold for visitors so they can see the next step more clearly without mistaking the test on the screen as a confirmation page. In future experimentation, CTF should continue to focus on creating a sense of urgency and emphasizing the importance of donor support for achieving its goals.

experiments.title: How removing an organization name from the sender line impacted opens
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the organization name’s from the sender for a housefile email will achieve an increase in open rates because the email will appear to be more humanized and personal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2023
We wanted to find out if removing the organization’s name from the sender line of an email would improve open rates. Our hypothesis was that the message will appear more personal and less like marketing if the sender line did not include an organization. We kept the sender’s title, however, to demonstrate credibility of the sender.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that removing the organization's name from the sender line of an email led to an increase in open rates. We saw a 7.4% increase in opens with 99% level of confidence. This suggests that people are more likely to open an email when they perceive it to be more personal and less like marketing. Therefore, in future experimentation, organizations can try to personalize their emails by removing formal or organizational language from the sender line. This can help to increase engagement with their target audience and improve the effectiveness of their email campaigns. However, it should be noted that this experiment only tested one aspect of an email and that other factors such as subject lines, content, and timing may also affect open rates. Therefore, future experimentation should look into optimizing these other factors as well.

experiments.title: How reducing the options on a gift array impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of gift array options for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there is less friction in the decision-making process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2023 - 03/09/2023
Because we had seen promising results on a similar test, we wanted to test reducing the number of options on a gift array for an organization’s French-speaking audience to see if they would also have an increase in conversion. The control donation page offered 5 donation amounts and an optional “Other” box to choose a gift of any amount. The treatment presented only a $10 ask and the “Other” field. We ran the test for 3 weeks splitting traffic evenly and measured results on donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the test for 3 weeks was the treatment produced a 116% lift in donations compared to the control with 96% level of confidence so the results are valid. We also saw a 136% lift in revenue with 98% level of confidence. However, we still had less than the minimum number of required transactions for these results to be statistically significant. We believe these results are due to the decrease in decision making because the donor does not have to spend additional time deciding how much to give. In future testing, we will want to test other gift amounts presented as an isolated option to see if the low $10 amount was the reason for the increase or if it was solely a result of reducing the number of options.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of options on a gift array impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of options on a gift array for petition signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there are fewer decisions to make at the point of conversion, reducing the amount of friction involved in the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2023 - 03/20/2023
In previous testing on other locations, we had seen an increase in donor response when asking for only one amount on the gift array. We wanted to test this again with a specific group of French-speaking petition signers on an instant donation page. We reduced the number of decisions involved by taking away all gift array options other than $10. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of options on a gift array for petition signers can effectively increase donor conversion rates, particularly for desktop visitors, returning visitors, and referral visitors. The treatment version, which only presented a single $10 donation option, resulted in a 120% increase in donations for all traffic with a 99.3% statistical level of confidence.
In future experimentation, the nonprofit should consider testing the impact of reducing the number of options on the gift array for other audience segments and for different donation amounts. It is also important to continually monitor results to ensure that certain segments are not negatively affected by the changes. We will also want to measure downstream conversion and lifetime value of these $10 donors and overall retention rate compared to other donors.

experiments.title: How simplifying a gift array impacted lapsed donor reactivation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that isolating a $10 ask on the gift array for lapsed donor survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2022 - 12/12/2022
We were running a survey to an audience of lapsed donors and followed the survey with an instant donation page. In previous testing, we had found that asking for a $10 gift in the body of the email copy increased donor conversion rate. We were interested in testing this same concept at the donation page level and hypothesized that reducing the number of choices on the gift array to only ask for a $10 gift would increase conversion rate from these lapsed donors. We removed all choices other than “$10” and “Other” from the gift array and split traffic equally between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed after running the experiment for 2 1/2 weeks was that there was a 79.8% increase in conversion rate with 99.5% level of confidence. We believe this is due to the reduced friction in the treatment experience where the donor did not have to pause and decide on an amount to give they could just decide "Do I want to give $10? Yes or no?" and continue their cognitive momentum from answering questions on the survey. We made the choice simple, reducing the perceived barrier in the decision-making process. While this treatment produced a better response in reactivating lapsed donors, in future testing we would like to test how this might impact new donor conversion as well. We also want to test increased gift amounts (such as $15 or $20) on the single gift array option to identify if it is the single choice or the lower amount that impacts results.

experiments.title: How reducing friction on a landing page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in petition signatures.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/19/2022 - 10/09/2022
When reviewing a petition landing page for possible barriers, we noticed there was a conflicting call to action on the page standing in the way of the email acquisition form: Click here for a printable version. We were curious if the link to the PDF was distracting users from completing the online form and was interrupting the cognitive forward momentum of page visitors. We designed a simple treatment: removing the interfering call to action from the landing page. We split traffic from 2 petitions 50/50 to this experience to observe what would happen to our email conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that reducing friction by removing the printable PDF link and call to action resulted in a 4.1% increase in email conversion rate with 99.4% level of confidence. Taking out this one step in the process led us to conversion lifts among all devices and all age segments of our audience. However, we also noticed a notable shift on a downstream metric that should be considered. Donations experienced a 48.5% decrease with a 94% level of confidence. We will need to run additional tests of this same concept on other petitions to see if these results are consistent. Due to this shift in donor response, it's possible that the printed version of the PDF is provided some sense of credibility to petition signers that is leading them to be more willing to convert on the instant donation page that follows it. If this is the case, we will need to test into an alternate method of displaying the option to access a printable version that does not interrupt the flow of the page or prohibit people from signing the petition.

experiments.title: How the placement of a lock icon impacted sign ups on a petition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the security of contact information for petition signers will achieve an increase in email conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2022 - 08/25/2022
We had previously run an experiment on petition pages that added a lock icon and security statement in the petition signature box on an email acquisition page but the results were flat and unclear if making this change had any impact on conversion. We wondered if the icon itself was too far removed from the security statement “We take data security and privacy seriously…” for page visitors to make the connection between the two elements. We created a new treatment that moved the padlock icon next to the privacy statement to enhance the safety of the information being requested for submission on the page in an effort to reduce anxiety and friction about giving away contact information. We were curious if emphasizing the commitment to security at the point of decision-making near the submit button would convince more people to convert.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the experiment for 2 weeks on multiple petition pages was that the treatment version led to a 6.9% increase in conversion rate with 98.1% level of confidence so the test results are valid. We saw increases in every audience segment across multiple devices. Because this was the only element that was altered, we are confident that the new placement on the page helped serve as a visual reminder of security that reduced visitor anxiety and made them more comfortable providing their contact information and clicking the submit button. Moving forward, we will update the icon placement for other active email acquisition offers to benefit from this lift in conversion.

experiments.title: How including a preview of a survey question in a Facebook ad impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in a Facebook ad graphic for potential survey takers will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/18/2022
We were running a survey offer on Facebook and wanted to develop a treatment to increase clickthrough rate. We wondered if replacing the stock imagery with a text-based ad that gave a preview of the first question on the survey would offer more clarity to ad viewers about what types of questions they would be asked to complete. We also changed the generic headline “Tell us what you think” to a more direct and specific CTA of “Take the survey” to offer additional clarity about the desired action we wanted them to take from the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running both versions of the ad was that the treatment design led to a 452% increase in clickthrough rate compared to the control with 100% level of confidence. Not only did we see a lift in the first step of the process, but we also saw a 307% increase in email acquisition. We weren't able to just send more people to visit the landing page with the treatment, we were also able to generate more survey responses. In addition to the lifts in both traffic and conversion, we also saw a 99% decrease in our cost per acquisition. We believe the additional clarity in the text-based ad reduced friction and confusion about the desired next step which made it easier for ad viewers to begin taking the survey and created cognitive momentum to complete the full process.

experiments.title: How emphasizing data security on a petition impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing data security for prospects signing a petition will achieve increase email signup rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2022 - 05/30/2022
As a part of continuous testing, the Canadian Taxpayer Federation wanted to try a simple message to emphasize data security on a petition to see how it would impact their email signup rate, donor conversion rate, and subsequently revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Much to our surprise, the treatment actually decreased email signup rates by -2.1% (LoC: 99%), but while we saw fewer email signups for prospects who were segmented into the treatment experience, we did see an increase in donor conversion rate of +102.9% (LoC: 98.9%) on the subsequent instant donation page.
We also observed an increase in revenue of +189.5% (with an LoC of 100%), as well.
The real question is: Why did this happen?
This experiment actually lines up with other experiments that increase security at the email signup rate that we've seen, where the email signup rate decreases slightly in exchange for a much higher donor conversion rate on the subsequent instant donation page.
The hypothesis as to why the email signup rate decreases is that it draws some attention to a prospect who otherwise wouldn't think that it was safe to provide their email address and cause a small percentage to reconsider the offer altogether.
Meanwhile, those that decide to proceed to feel safer in the decision to make a gift on the subsequent instant donation page, because they already know that you take the safety and security of their personal information seriously, and it's through this increase in trust that they'll decide to give at a higher rate.
This result really just simply comes down to the principle of "decreasing anxiety" (which is defined as "a psychological to take the presented action") within the mind of the prospective donor by letting them know that they can trust you.
Our upcoming experiment with this treatment is to increase the visual appearance of security on the email signup form along with the disclaimer language designed to heighten the perception of security to even greater levels.

experiments.title: How shortening a matching gift announcement impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the matching gift language and making it more direct for non-donors will achieve a donor conversion and revenue increase.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2022 - 03/13/2022
A generous donor made a matching gift available to the Canadian Taxpayer Federation so that they could specifically inspire non-donors to become first-time donors.
They wanted to know how a matching gift announcement for non-donors who just signed a petition would react and whether or not the matching gift would increase giving for this audience.
Initially, we tested a message that took its time to explain the importance of the match and why the donor should take advantage of it. Based upon previous research on the Taxpayer donor audience, we hypothesized that shortening the message and being more direct about the existence of the match would be the first test that we should try to inspire non-donors to make their first gift (and have it matched by this generous donor).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 98.8%, we observed an increase in dollars raised by +23.4% — all from visitors who had no giving history to the organization! This revenue lift was driven by two factors, which are:
We saw an increase of 16.9% in donor conversion rate (people saying "yes" to make a gift of any amount), andWhen someone decided to give through the treatment, they decided to give a gift that was 5.2% higher than donors in the control.
So, not only did we get more people to say yes, but when they did, they gave more generously — all because of the matching gift message.
It's important to note that in a previous experiment, we tested a variant of the matching gift announcement with the same visual appearance and placement on the donation form (and on the same pages), but that variant actually decreased donor conversion rate by -32%.
If we would have stopped there, it's very possible that we would not have been able to inspire these first-time donors to say "yes" at a higher rate and gift amount.
It's our hypothesis that the overly verbose announcement of the matching gift in the first test variant was long enough to cause a wrap, and was a paragraph that used multiple sentences to say what this variation said in a single line of text and in one sentence.
The key learning here is this: When announcing a matching gift, get straight to the point. Spending too much time trying to justify why someone should make a gift to have it matched by another donor may not be necessary.
By communicating too much at this point in the giving experience, it's very likely that we introduce anxiety — or "psychological resistance" — to the visitors to this page to say "yes" to making a gift at all.
By shortening the copy and being more direct, we mitigated that resistance, and unlocked generosity that lifted overall results.

experiments.title: How using a drop down option instead of an open text field on a calculator impacted donor conversion rate

experiments.keyLearnings:

experiments.title: How breaking your campaign goal down for non-donors impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that breaking down the fundraising goal into $10 ask amounts for non-donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2022 - 02/18/2022
As a part of continued testing and experimentation related to what drives the best response rates within the context of a high urgency fundraising campaign from non-donors, the Canadian Taxpayer Federation wanted to test an “open ask” against the $10 ask for non-donors to verify whether or not the specific ask amount would drive results if it was the only variant.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We observed a +74.1% increase in donor conversion rate (with a 97.4% level of confidence) with the treatment experience.
Furthermore, we noted an increase in revenue of +141.1% (with a 99.4% level of confidence).
Like with the other study, we found that the control experience only produce just one $100+ donor, while the treatment experience actually produced 9 $100+ donors.
Why? Our thought is that the $10 ask amount for a non-donor makes it easy for someone to click through to the donation page and that the decision to give more is a combination of both (a) a good gift array, and (b) compelling donation page copy (that builds momentum for donors to give more).
Overall, it appears that the $10 ask amount is the way to go with non-donors, as this verifies the lift that we saw with previous experiences of a similar experience.

experiments.title: How the amount you ask for in your campaign impacts your non-donor response rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ask amount and donor goal drives the best response rates from non-donors within the context of a fundraising campaign?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2022
As the Canadian Taxpayer Federation continued experimenting with breaking their campaign goal down into smaller, more achievable goals, they wanted to experiment with what drove the greatest non-donor response rates.
To experiment with what drives the best response rates, they broke their campaign goal and ask amounts into four groups.
Those groups were:
$10
$25
$50
$100
They then built a treatment like a previous email used for the same campaign that broke down the fundraising goal into one of those four ask amounts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In total, asking in this way showed an aggregate lift of +126.7% in donor conversion rate (with a 100% level of confidence).
There's no doubt that asking in this way drives non-donors to respond at a higher rate (more than 2x when compared to the control experience).
In terms of what ask amount drives the greatest response and revenue, as shown above, the evidence points to asking for $10 from non-donors (since that had the largest lift — +270.3% in donor conversion rate, fully validated with a confidence score of 99.9%).
But the question is: Are we leaving money on the table by asking for so little?
The answer? ABSOLUTELY NOT.
Here is how revenue increased when compared to the control ask experience across the various ask amount segments:
$10 Ask Amount: +147.7% (LoC: 99.3%)$25 Ask Amount: +97.5% (LoC: 97.5%)$50 Ask Amount: +124.1% (LoC: 98.1%)$100 Ask Amount: +49.7% (LoC: 83.9%)
The only ask category that didn't fully validate as an increase from the ask amounts listed above was the $100 ask amount category, but there is directional data that points towards that lifting results, as well.
Additionally, here is how the percentage of gifts and revenue were spread across the various ask amount categories within the treatment segment:
$10 Ask Amount: 41% of gifts & 32% of revenue raised in the treatment segment$25 Ask Amount: 24% of gifts & 24% of revenue raised in the treatment segment$50 Ask Amount: 21% of gifts & 27% of revenue raised in the treatment segment$100 Ask Amount: 13% of gifts & 17% of revenue raised in the treatment segment
This means that asking for $10 from non-donors not only generated the greatest response rate but also drove the largest share of gifts and revenue.
Finally, we wanted to check to see if we received a disproportionately lower number of $100+ gifts in the lower ask segments of the treatment. Here is what we found: The $10 ask amount produced 5/12432 (or 0.04% of gifts) at the $100 gift level, while the $100 ask segment produced just 6/12476 (or 0.05% of gifts).
Simply put, this means that asking for $10 from non-donors produced just as many $100+ gifts, while also producing 3.1x the number of gifts compared to the number secured within the $100 ask segment, and 1.9x the revenue from that segment, as well.
All of this is to say: It appears that asking for $10 not only activated the highest number of donors but it raised the most money of any ask amount from non-donors.
Further experimentation is advised for your program, but at least for the CTF — asking non-donors to give $10 to a high urgency campaign effort is very much the way to go moving forward.

experiments.title: How breaking down your campaign goal impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that breaking your campaign goal down into smaller target gift amounts for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/04/2022
As a part of a campaign designed to raise money to run radio ads about a new legislative initiative, the Canadian Taxpayer Federation wanted to better understand how best to ask their donors and subscribers to contribute to their campaign.
In an attempt to do something radically different than how they have asked in the past for short-term, project-based fundraising goals, they decided to break their goal down into smaller amounts and publish the number of gifts that it would take at that smaller target goal per donor to make the larger fundraising goal seem more realistic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed an increase in revenue of +81.1%!
We also observed an increase of +103.7% in the donor conversion rate metric (which also validated with a 100% level of confidence).
Our hypothesis as to why the treatment worked so much better than the control is because large fundraising goals make smaller dollar donors feel like the problem or burden to achieve that goal will need to be solved by a donor with much, much larger capacity to give a really large gift. Additionally, it could also make the small-dollar donor feel shame that the smaller gift that they're capable of giving today isn't "good enough" to help the organization—and because of that—they decide to not give a gift at all.
The breakdown of the larger fundraising goal into a smaller gift amount not only drove more than twice the number of gifts created by the control experience but what's really interesting is that the average gift between the two segments only decreased by 11% when compared to the control segment.
Finally, it's worth noting a few more observations, which include:
43% of the treatment donors gave more than what they were asked for and were worth 79% of the funds raised in the segment.42% of the treatment donors gave what they were asked for and were worth 17% of the funds raised in the segment.Only 16% of the treatment donors gave less than the amount they were asked for and were only worth 5% of the funds raised in the segment.While the control segment drove a higher percentage (21.5%) of donors at/above $100 when compared to the treatment (17.6%), the overall number of $100+ donors increased by 65% within the treatment segment.
Our recommendation is to use this campaign goal breakdown approach moving forward for all big fundraising campaigns.
More experimentation could be done in terms of setting daily goals for the campaign or making the ask amount to an amount that is relevant to a donor's most recent gift (or highest gift) or setting a smaller number of donors that need to achieve a gift smaller gift amount (and note that you'll combine those gifts with others to achieve the overall goal).

Further experimentation may be required to know the impact of donor conversion rate on other offers/petitions, however, this approach seems to drive a higher volume of clickers, which is flowing into a higher volume of email signers, as well.

experiments.title: How social proof impacted petition signature rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would “joining a community of X people who have already signed this petition” increase petition signature rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2020 - 10/17/2020
As a part of Canadian Taxpayer’s efforts to increase petition signature rates, we decided to experiment with using social proof to create momentum for those considering signing the petition. We decided to show petition page viewers how many people had already signed the petition they were considering signing.
Would “joining a community of X people who have already signed this petition” increase petition signature rate?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed a +3.2% increase in petition signature rate.
It appears that showing how many people have already signed the petition drives a slight increase in the overall petition signature rate.
There was some thought that the smaller numbers (early on) for the treatment might cause it to trail the control variant within the experiment. However, upon further review, we observed the following by looking at each quarter of the experiment:
In the first 13 days of the experiment, the treatment led by +4.9% (with a level of confidence of 97.3%).In the next 13 days of the experiment, the treatment trailed by -2.5% (with a level of confidence of 74.3%).In the following 13 days of the experiment, the treatment led by +1.1% (with a level of confidence of 57.2%).In the final 13 days of the experiment, the treatment led by +2.7% (with a level of confidence of 94.5%).
Based upon this information, we saw that in the first 13 days, a valid lift right from the start for this treatment (feasibly, when the numbers for those who signed the petition would be at their lowest). As petition signatures continued to grow, it appears that the petition signature rate did increase, as well (albeit just short of fully validating at the target 95% level of confidence).
Executive Summary: It appears that "joining a growing community" seems to slightly increase the petition signature rate, especially as time goes by, but doesn't necessarily negatively impact a petition when it's first launched (and the petition signature totals are small).

experiments.title: How promoting a gift premium impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would expanding the value proposition of the incentive increase donors at least at the qualifying gift level without negatively impacting the overall donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/02/2020
The Canadian Taxpayer Federation offers donors at the $100 gift level a print magazine called “Taxpayer Magazine” as a premium (or incentive) to give at or above that level. We wondered whether or not expanding the value proposition of the incentive would increase donors at or above that level, without negatively impacting the overall donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 98.1% level of confidence, we observed an overall donor conversion rate decrease of -44.8% for the treatment.
Unfortunately, expanding the value proposition of the incentive offer did decrease the donor conversion rate overall. However, we did observe a valid increase (98.6% level of confidence) of +196% at the $100+ gift level (which the premium was offered at).
Even with this increase of donors at a much higher average gift level—we did observe a near-directional decrease in revenue of -21% (the level of confidence was only 78%).
Because of the revenue drop overall, the organization decided to take the higher volume of donors (and overall higher revenue total) of the control experience, rather than the fewer (but more valuable) donors through the treatment experience.
In reviewing the treatment and results, our hypothesis is that the overall donor conversion rate was down for the treatment because donors who weren't capable of giving at that level felt that their gift wasn't valued if not at that level.
Perhaps finding another way to expand the value proposition of Taxpayer Magazine could keep the overall donor conversion rate while also increasing the rate of $100+ gift donors that are capable of giving at that level.

They wondered if updating the header image to align with the person in which the letter was written to would increase the petition signature (email signup) rate.

experiments.title: How removing branding and social proof to simplify a petition impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing branding (and the corresponding social proof) impact email signup, donor conversion or revenue rates?

Ended On: 08/20/2020
As a part of optimizing petitions used for the acquisition of new activists and donors, the Canadian Taxpayer Federation looked to experiment with the branding, which also included a social proof element, from the petition signing page. How would this impact email signup rate, and downstream donor and revenue metrics?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 98.3% level of confidence, we observed a decrease in instant donor conversion rate of 65.7% when the organization’s logo and the corresponding social proof was removed from the top of the petition signature page.
There are two other interesting observations, which are:
The email signup rate slightly increased — showing a +2.8% (LoC: 96.7%), but the decrease in subsequent donor conversion rate also drove a …
Decrease in revenue of -60.6% (LoC: 95.4%).
Could the organization’s branding and social proof (showing the size of the community they would be joining if they made a gift on the subsequent donation page after signing the petition) decrease their likelihood to give a gift at all?
Ultimately, we decided to turn off the experiment because the increase in email signup rates did not jump high enough to offset the big decreases we witnessed in donor conversion and revenue.
Further experimentation may be required, but it certainly appears that when you have social proof to publicize, that it is far more important to publicize it all of the way through the funnel instead of removing it, as it appears to have negatively impacted subsequent giving.

experiments.title: How simplifying petition language impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Would simplifying the contents of the open letter (by decreasing the evaluated “reading grade level” from 10th grade down to a 6th grade reading level) impact letter signature rates, donor conversion or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/23/2020
The Canadian Taxpayers Federation (CTF) is a federally incorporated, not-for-profit citizen’s group dedicated to lower taxes, less waste and accountable government.
As a part of their efforts to urge the Canadian federal government in their efforts to provide relief to Canadian citizens in response to COVID-19 (coronavirus), they put together an open letter campaign that allowed Canadian citizens to urge their lawmakers for fiscal responsibility in response to the pandemic.
The open letter was followed by an instant donation page which provided open letter signers to make a gift to help reach more people to sign the open letter.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With 95.2% level of confidence, we observed a +90.8% increase in revenue for open letter signers who became donors in the subsequent instant donation page.
This increase was driven by two metrics, specifically:
An increase in donor conversion rate for treatment signers by +22.5% (although the LoC was only 66.5% and did not validate), and
The average gift for donors in the treatment experience was +59.6% higher than the control donors.
Ironically, the email signup rate was rather unaffected by the treatment (improving only 0.7%, with a LoC of 25.8%).
Key Takeaways:
When you read the two treatments, the simplification of the letter content didn’t really “take anything away” from the content—in fact, the simplification made the letter appear more direct to the lawmaker it was being sent to. Our theory is that this increased “directness” of the message was a better representation of the reader/signer’s message, and when presented with an ask, they gave more generously on the instant donation page.
Because the organization was looking to maximize return on investment, the treatment was rolled out to 100% of the audience. Further experimentation may be required to verify if this approach could work again in the future.
