experiments.title: How humanizing a dear reader impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a human invitation for website visitors will achieve an increase in newsletter signups because we know that people tend to respond to things that are “alive” in some way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2024 - 05/07/2024
When launching a newsletter and advertising it to site visitors below blogs/articles on the webpage, we wondered if we could replicate the success we had seen through other orgnization’s testing of effectively increasing email acquisition by humanizing our communications. We developed a treatment that leaned into the humanity of the newsletter offer and that emphasized that you’re going to hear directly from Franco—an insider on the latest happening inside the Canadian government if you sign up for weekly newsletter updates. We ran this experiment for 4 weeks to determine just how adding a clear value proposition and highlighting a familiar face from your organization can improve response.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a human invitation for website visitors can significantly increase email acquisition for the Canadian Taxpayers Federation's newsletter. The treatment, which emphasized hearing directly from Franco—an insider on the latest happenings inside the Canadian government, led to a 57.6% increase in email signups compared to the control group.

While the results of this experiment are promising, the statistical level of confidence of 90.9% falls slightly below the standard threshold of 95% for validity. This suggests that further testing may be necessary to confirm that the treatment consistently has the intended effect.

In future experimentation, the Canadian Taxpayers Federation should continue to explore the impact of humanizing communications and highlighting familiar faces within the organization to engage website visitors. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations of the human invitation, such as featuring other insiders or providing specific examples of the type of content subscribers can expect to receive. By iteratively testing and optimizing these approaches, the organization can continue to improve email acquisition and overall engagement with its audience.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to petition summaries led to increase in emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding descriptions to the petition summary cards for non-paid website traffic will lead to an increase in petition signatures because it adds more clarity and strengthens the motivation of the user clicking through.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2024 - 04/17/2024
In this experiment, Canadian Taxpayers Federation was exploring how they might optimize the top of their petition funnel. Historically they have spent a lot of time and effort on optimizing paid ad campaigns driving traffic to specific petition offers. But they wondered if they could drive more motivated visitors to petition pages from their existing website traffic.
Their original petitions landing page had lots of summary cards showing the title of the petition, a representative image, and a “Sign the Petition” call-to-action button.
They wondered if adding brief descriptions to the petition summary cards would give more context to the user and ultimately lead to more petition signers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the brief descriptions to the petition summaries led to a 17% increase in petition signatures.

 

While historically this page has not been a significant driver of petition signatures, this experiment shows that the potential is there to drive more motivated users into a key acquisition funnel. It just requires some optimization to understand what will drive motivated click-throughs from existing website traffic.

 

Future experimentation should focus on template-level ideas that can increase the clarity of these petition offers as well as drive more motivated traffic through to the next step.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of options on a gift array impacted non-donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of options on a gift array for nondonors will achieve an increase in conversion because there are fewer options to consider thereby reducing decision friction in the donation process. because  there are fewer options to consider thereby reducing decision friction in the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/09/2024
We wondered if having a $200 option on the gift array for nondonors was causing friction in the decision making process. We set up a test removing the highest amount from the gift array to help the donation ask feel more accessible to someone who has never made a gift to CTF before and reduce the decision friction by having too many options to consider.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that by reducing the number of options on a gift array for non-donors on a post-survey donation page, there was a 14.4% directional increase in donations for all traffic at 91% level of confidence. This suggests that having fewer options to consider can reduce decision friction in the donation process and potentially increase conversion rates.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the impact of simplifying the donation process by offering fewer options on the gift array. Further testing is necessary to confirm the results and determine if this approach consistently leads to higher donation rates. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations in donation amounts to see which ones resonate best with potential donors and drive the highest conversion rates.

experiments.title: How having short direct copy impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having shorter copy for all users will achieve increase donations because they could skip the copy more quickly. because they could skip the copy more quickly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/07/2022 - 07/15/2022
Wrong images
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The hypothesis that shorter copy would lead to increased donations was proven wrong. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group.

2. The statistical level of confidence for the overall results is below the threshold of 85%, which means the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

3. However, it is worth noting that there was a significant decrease in donations from visitors aged 25-34, with a 99% level of confidence. This suggests that the shorter copy may have had a negative impact on this specific demographic.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the hypothesis before conducting the experiment. The result of this experiment indicates that shorter copy may not always lead to increased donations, so it would be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence donation behavior.

Additionally, it may be worth conducting further research or experiments specifically targeting visitors aged 25-34 to better understand their behavior and preferences. This could help in creating more effective strategies and messaging for this particular demographic.

experiments.title: How a humanized donation page impacted gifts from survey takers
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing a donation page for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the message is aligned from a relevant staff member and positioned as a personal request.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2023 - 07/05/2023
On an instant donation page after visitors completed a survey, we wondered if the value proposition message would resonate more strongly with potential donors if it was presented in the first person. Since the survey was relevant to investigative journalism, we tested writing the donation page language as a message from an investigative journalist “Ryan” and signing the donation page with his name as if it were a personal message to the reader. We positioned this against the winning control from our previous test – using language around the principle of scarcity and referencing that only 1% of survey takers would take an invested next step toward change by making a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a personal message from an investigative journalist did not resonate more strongly with potential donors. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 12% decrease in donations compared to the control with 83% level of confidence.

This experiment suggests that potential donors may not respond favorably to a personal message from a staff member. It is important to carefully consider the messaging and tone used in donation pages to ensure that they are effective in motivating donors to take action.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to explore other messaging strategies that may be more effective in driving donations. It may also be valuable to further analyze the specific group of new visitors who saw a significant decrease in donations and determine if there are any underlying factors that contributed to this outcome.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing and iterating on donation page messaging to continually optimize and improve conversion rates. Since this was a radical redesign from the existing copy, we could also pair the 1% language as a humanized message from Ryan going forward and see if pairing the winning message with an isolated variable makes a positive impact on conversion rate.

experiments.title: How positioning match oriented language impacted recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity around a matching gift campaign for recurring donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/26/2023
During a recurring gift campaign, we wanted to test which message would be more motivating to donors to upgrade from a one time to a recurring gift. On the control page, we highlighted that a matching gift was in place which would double their donation. For the treatment donation page, we went into more detail about the match and focused the value proposition more around doubling the impact of their monthly donation. We split traffic to each page 50/50 for the duration of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that adding more clarity around a matching gift campaign for recurring donors did not achieve the expected increase in donor conversion. In fact, the treatment page resulted in a 26.5% decrease in donations with a statistical level of confidence just below the acceptable threshold. Therefore, further testing is necessary before making any definitive conclusions about the effectiveness of messaging related to matching gift campaigns for recurring donors. We learned that the match language—on its own—was less effective in converting donors than keeping the values-based language and mentioning the match as one additional incentive to give. In future experimentation, it may be helpful to focus on more targeted messaging and testing smaller changes in order to better understand the impact on donor behavior. Additionally, it may be worthwhile to test other types of incentives beyond matching gifts to determine which strategies are most effective in motivating donors to convert to recurring gifts.

experiments.title: How validating each form field impacted overall completion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding form field validation for petition signers will achieve an increase in petition signatures because the checkmarks will reduce friction by adding cognitive momentum and clarity in the user process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2022 - 12/16/2022
When reviewing petition signature pages, we wondered what how we could create a treatment that would provide additional cognitive momentum to reward visitors for beginning the process and encourage them to complete the remaining steps. We developed a green checkmark that would appear when someone had completed a required field to let them know they had successfully completed that step and could move on to the next field. If a user missed a step on either the control or the treatment, they could clearly see an error message that would prompt them to return to that form field and correctly complete it, but the treatment also interjected a “Looks good!” message to confirm they could keep moving forward. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment were that adding form field validation did not result in an increase in petition signatures for all traffic. However, the treatment did lead to a significant increase in revenue with a 98% level of confidence. Moreover, the treatment showed mixed results across different visitor segments. For instance, there was a decrease in signups for mobile and email visitors, a significant increase in tablet visitors, and a considerable decrease in signups for female and male visitors.

Given these results, it is important to keep in mind that implementing form field validation may not improve petition signatures for all segments of visitors. Therefore, future experimentation should focus on identifying which visitor segments may benefit from this treatment and which segments may not. Additionally, the revenue increase observed in the treatment group should be further investigated to understand the underlying factors contributing to this effect. Lastly, it is worthwhile to continue experimenting with different design treatments that can reduce friction and encourage visitors to complete the signup process.

experiments.title: How using images of politicians impacted petition signatures
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using images of well-known politicians for petition signers will achieve an increase in signatures because the motivation to sign would be more heavily impacted by emotion by association.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2023 - 01/28/2023
On a petition to repeal alcohol tax, we wondered if instead of using a standard image of the issue itself, how petition signers would respond to images of the politicians in the decision-making seats who were the ones implementing the policies. Our hypothesis was that the treatments that used politician images would invoke more emotion from visitors which might fuel their momentum to complete the petition. We split traffic 3 ways and let the test run for 16 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using images of famous politicians for petition signers did not achieve the intended increase in signatures. The control version of the petition outperformed the treatment with a statistical level of confidence approaching validity. The use of politician images did not motivate visitors to sign the petition, and some categories of visitors, such as social, female, and 25-34 age group, had a significant decrease in signatures with a high level of confidence. Desktop and other visitors had an increase in signatures, but these results were not consistent across all traffic.

In future experimentation, it may be necessary to test the use of images of politicians in a more targeted way, such as using images of politicians who are in favor of repealing the alcohol tax. Also, it may be useful to test different types of emotional appeals, beyond the association with politicians, to motivate visitors to sign the petition. The language used in both versions of the petition was similar, so testing different wording may also be beneficial for future experiments. It is also important to consider the characteristics and preferences of different visitor segments when implementing new designs or strategies.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted gifts on a post-petition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using more you-focused language for petition signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the emphasis is less on the organization and more about what the donor wants to see happen.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 12/12/2022
When evaluating our standard donation page language presented to someone after they signed a petition, we noticed the language was very organization-centric and we wondered if the value proposition could be reconstructed. The control copy says “Would you consider donating so we can show this petition to people on social media?” and we wondered if this was what the donor actually wanted to do or if we could make a more compelling case for support using more donor-centric language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment revealed that using more donor-centric language for petition signers resulted in a 15.4% increase in donations with an 89.6% level of confidence. While the statistical level of confidence is below the 95% threshold, the results are approaching validity, implying that further testing is necessary to confirm its effectiveness. Additionally, the (Other) visitors had an 86% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

The key takeaway from this experiment is that a shift towards more you-focused language is likely to increase donor conversion rates. Future experimentation should focus on refining the language to gain further improvements in the results. Based on these insights, Canadian Taxpayers Federation can improve its donation conversion rates by creating more compelling donor-centric value propositions.

experiments.title: How a matching gift announcement impacted donor conversion rate for non-donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that announcing a matching gift for non-donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2022 - 02/06/2022
A generous donor made a matching gift available to the Canadian Taxpayer Federation so that they could specifically inspire non-donors to become first-time donors.
They wanted to know how a matching gift announcement for non-donors who just signed a petition would react and whether or not the matching gift would increase giving for this audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With an 83.3% level of confidence, we observed a decrease in donor conversion rate of -28.7% for non-donors on acquisition donation forms.
Other observations observed in this experiment include:
Tablet visitors had an 88.8% decrease in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 67.4% decrease in revenue with a 93% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that this message is perhaps too long and not targeted enough. Therefore, we will be re-building this experiment to try another treatment that meets these needs.

experiments.title: How starting the gift array at a higher level impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the first gift array option for petition signers will achieve increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2021 - 02/13/2021
The Canadian Taxpayer Federation was continuing their experimentation with their gift arrays on post-petition donation forms, and decided to experiment with a higher starting point on the gift array.
The control experience started with a $10 option and the treatment started with a $25 gift option.
Would increasing the amount on the first gift array button increase the donor conversion rate and raise revenue?
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment near fully validated but was turned off early because there was a near-valid decrease in donor conversion rate of -27.5% (LoC: 93.4%).

Some other observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 54.3% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 28.0% decrease in donations with a 90% level of confidence.
The treatment decreased gifts by -83% and revenue by -85% at the $10 (or below) gift level.
 	The treatment increased gifts by +23% and revenue by 23% at the $25 gift level. And,
 	After removing a large outlier gift from the treatment, we saw that it decreased gifts by -30% and revenue by -40% at the $25+ gift level.

experiments.title: How targeted digital advertising affected direct mail results
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing ads to a targeted group of direct mail recipients result in increased revenue from the treatment group?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/26/2017 - 11/25/2017
The Canadian Taxpayers Federation wanted to test whether showing Facebook video ads to house file direct mail recipients, before and during the response period caused a lift in any key metric: conversion, donation size or total revenue. This test was based on the successful test executed by Hillsdale College.
The Taxpayers Federation created a control audience that was excluded from seeing any video ads, and a treatment audience that would see a rotation of three video ads (but no donation-centric or petition ads) for a one week period before the mail piece hit mailboxes and for three weeks after, the prime time in which a donor would respond. They chose videos that had been popular in the past, but were still relevant. They optimized the ads for reach—aiming to show ads to as large a percentage of the target group as possible. The goal of this test was to lift direct mail revenue—not to add an additional segment of online revenue or capture data through petitions.
Ads were turned off once each video had an average frequency (number of times shown to each person reached) greater than 4.1. However, some videos were displayed as many as 4.5 times.
They spent just shy of $700 (CDN) to show ads to the treatment audience and waited to see the results come in.
experiments.keyLearnings: It appears that with a 25.4% increase in dollars per visitor, at a 92.6% confidence level, that this was a success and should be expanded and replicated in the future. Overall, a $690 investment in Facebook ads, resulted in a nearly $10,000 increase in overall revenue.

experiments.title: How adding a banner to a donation page with a button to accelerate the process impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to begin the donation for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there will be reduced friction present for visitors who are already motivated to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2024 - 03/04/2024
For highly motivated traffic coming to a main donation page from the homepage donate button in the top right corner, we hypothesized that giving them a chance to skip over the copy on the donation form and go straight to the gift frequency and gift array selections without having to read the donation page would reduce friction in the process that we know they initiated by arriving on this page. We had tested into having shorter copy on this page to help get the form above the fold, but wondered how else we could escalate the process and reduce the work involved for visitors here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to begin the donation process for main donation page visitors can potentially increase donor conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 6.7% increase in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical confidence level of 72.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that the results may not be reliable and should not be solely relied upon for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore ways to reduce friction for motivated visitors on the donation page. This could include streamlining the donation process, providing clearer calls to action, or optimizing the layout of the page to make it easier for visitors to navigate and complete their donation. It is important to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to improve donor conversion rates, while also ensuring that the results are statistically valid before implementing any changes permanently.

experiments.title: How preselecting the first amount on the gift array impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting the gift array amount for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because there is enhanced cognitive momentum on the page reducing friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2023 - 12/28/2023
For a high traffic donation page, we wanted to test if pre-selecting an amount for the visitor would encourage more visitors to complete the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that pre-selecting the gift array amount on the donation page may have a positive impact on the conversion rate. The treatment group, which had the pre-selected amount, saw a 12.8% increase in transactions compared to the control group.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 59.2%, which is below the desired threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

To further understand the impact of pre-selecting the gift array amount, it is recommended to conduct additional experiments with larger sample sizes and longer durations. This will help to increase the statistical power and confidence level of the results, providing more reliable insights.

Future experimentation should also consider other factors that could influence the conversion rate, such as the visibility and placement of the pre-selected amount, as well as the impact on different segments of visitors. By conducting rigorous experiments and analyzing the results with statistical validity, the organization can make informed decisions regarding the implementation of pre-selected amounts for donation pages.

experiments.title: How social proof vs. credibility impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a statement of credibility for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will build trust in the value proposition and give the donor more reasons to believe our claims.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2023 - 06/22/2023
In a previous test, we saw that adding a quote from a current volunteer board member produced a lift in donations. We wondered if adding a statement of credibility using the same format would make any difference in conversion. We pulled a quote from Forbes magazine commending the organization and put it in the same placement on the page as an element of social proof right before the donation form and split traffic 50/50, sending half of visitors to the control with Ken’s quote about donating and the other half to the treatment with the Forbes magazine call-out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment aimed to determine if adding a statement of credibility before the donation form would increase donor conversion. The treatment included a quote from Forbes magazine commending the organization, while the control had a quote from a volunteer board member. The experiment ran for 28 days, and the results showed that the treatment had a 53.8% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.9% and thus cannot be considered valid.

From this experiment, we can learn that adding a statement of credibility may not always lead to an increase in donor conversion. It is essential to conduct further experimentation to confirm the validity of the initial results. What we saw was the audience was more motivated by a donor-centric quote from a supporter regarding their experience with giving to the organization than they were by the organizational-centric quote from a review that gave an exclusive reason to support the organization. One reason for this could be the difference in the positioning of the language in the quotes "Let's go get 'em" which insinuates there is work left to be done versus the treatment that claims the organization has the "largest communication presence" which could lead donors to believe that the organization does not need their help or support since they are already doing the most/best with their existing resources and are already in a position to be successful without the donor making a gift to help.

experiments.title: How presentation of a sender name impacted open rates on a survey
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the organization’s full name from the sender line for survey takers will achieve an increase in open rates because the email recipients will believe it is from a real person, not an organization.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2023
In previous testing we had seen a lift in removing the organization’s name from the sender line but we wanted to test this same concept with a larger audience. For the control, we included both title and organization name. For the treatment, we used the organization’s initials and the sender title.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that removing the organization's full name from the sender line for survey takers may result in a slight increase in open rates. However, since the statistical level of confidence is 31%, the results cannot be considered statistically valid. Therefore, it is recommended to conduct further testing with larger sample sizes to validate the results before making any decisions based on them. We did also see a 6.9% decrease in clicks on the treatment with 78.9% level of confidence, so we need to do further testing to see how the sender name could have impacted clickthrough rate conversion.

experiments.title: How conversational headers on a donation form impacted conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that creating conversational headers for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because people will treat purely digital experiences like they are alive in some way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 12/09/2022
When reviewing a donation form post-petition, we realized the conversational nature of the donation page language ended abruptly once we began introducing different sections of the forms with headers. The form was broken up by standard titles “Donation amount” “Enter your information” “Credit card information” but we wondered if treating these steps in the process as a place to continue the conversation with the donor would help humanize the process and improve donor conversion on the page. We developed a treatment that guided visitors through each step on the page. We introduced the following treatment headers “Step 1: Would you like to make a one-time donation or support this work monthly?” “Step 2: How much would you like to give?” “Step 3: What is your billing information?” and “Step 4: What’s your payment information?” and split traffic 50/50 across a handful of donation forms.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that creating conversational headers on a donation page can result in a small increase in donor conversion. However, the statistical level of confidence for the overall traffic was not significant enough to be considered valid. A more significant learning is that the treatment had a significant impact on gender and specific visitor groups. Female visitors had a substantial increase in donations while other visitors had a significant decrease. This information can be used to inform future experiments by emphasizing conversational language for female visitors and potentially exploring other treatment options for other visitor groups. It is essential to note that further testing would need to be done to validate these findings and make data-driven decisions.

experiments.title: How presenting a single option in a gift array for video viewers impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because people will have to spent less time considering which choice to make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2022 - 12/25/2022
In a series of testing around gift array options, we wanted to test the impact on donor conversion and revenue by removing the extraneous choices from the gift array menu and offering visitors one option to select to donate $10 or type in the amount of their own choosing in the “Other” field. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and ran the test for two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers increased donor conversion by 16.0%. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 62.8%, which means the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making. Future experiments should be designed with larger sample sizes and longer testing periods to ensure statistical significance. Additionally, tests should be conducted on different target audiences to see if these results hold true for other groups. Overall, the experiment suggests that simplifying the donation process may lead to increased donations, but further testing is needed to confirm this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How eliminating a confirmation message post-survey impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that drawing attention to the donation page copy for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will keep their eyes on the donation page longer rather than leaving immediately upon arrival.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/26/2022
After submitting a survey that led to an instant donation page, we wondered if people were reading the confirmation message “YOU DID IT! Your survey is submitted.” and automatically thinking they had no other action to take on the page and they could click to exit without reading the donation ask immediately following their submission. We developed a treatment that removed the confirmation message and instead led with the headline “One More Thing…” to indicate that their participation in the process was not fully complete. We still mention in the opening paragraph that they filled out the survey and thank them for their time and input. We ran these two versions side by side and measured the impact on donor conversion over a 10 day period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of the A/B experiment showed that the treatment version, which removed the confirmation message and led with the headline "One More Thing", had a 10.7% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control version, with a statistical level of confidence of 40.2%. Although the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results suggest that drawing attention to the donation page copy can increase donor conversion rate by keeping their eyes on the page longer.

In future experimentation, we can try to modify the treatment version and test it again to achieve a higher statistical level of confidence. We can also try to identify other factors that might affect donor conversion rate and run further experiments to optimize the donation process. It is essential to keep testing and optimizing the donation page and messaging to increase the number of donations and maximize the impact of the nonprofit's work.

experiments.title: How a simpler gift array impacted gifts on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating decision friction for nondonors after submitting a survey will achieve an increase in $10 donations because the body copy will have synergy with the donation form elements.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2022 - 12/31/2022
When sending non-donors through a survey to an instant donation page, we wondered if removing the one time vs. monthly option and reducing the gift array from multiple options to mirror the ask in the donation page language for 3,000 supporters to donate $10 would improve conversion rate. We split traffic between the two versions and ran the experiment for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that eliminating decision friction for non-donors after submitting a survey can result in an increase in $10 donations. The treatment saw a 15.7% increase in donations for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was only 69.3%, which is below the threshold for valid results. Therefore, the findings cannot be used for future decision making. However, the experiment provides a starting point for future experimentation to optimize the donation process, such as further testing of the language and design of the donation page. The results highlight the importance of carefully evaluating statistical significance and not relying on results below the threshold for valid experimentation.

experiments.title: How relevant images impacted petition signatures
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image from the landing page for petition signers will achieve an increase in signature conversions because there would be less friction on the page by removing a distracting or emotional element.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2023 - 01/15/2023
On a petition to repeal beer tax, we wondered if the image on the landing page was distracting or dissuading visitors from completing the petition. We created a treatment that removed the image altogether, and developed a second treatment that portrayed the politician the petition would be submitted to. We split traffic between the three versions and let the experiment run for 5 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing an image from the landing page did not result in an increase in signature conversions. In fact, there was a slight decrease in the number of sign-ups. However, this result cannot be considered valid due to a low statistical level of confidence. It is also important to note that there were significant differences in sign-up rates among different devices and traffic sources. Desktop visitors showed a significant decrease in sign-ups, while tablet and email visitors showed a significant increase.

In future experimentation, it would be important to examine these differences among devices and traffic sources and to tailor the landing page accordingly. It may also be useful to test different messaging strategies to see if there is a specific type of language or tone that resonates better with potential signers. Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of careful testing and analysis to ensure that decisions are informed by valid and reliable data.

experiments.title: How reducing the options on a gift array impacted response from non-donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the gift array options for nondonors will achieve an increase in instant donor conversion because we are eliminating friction in the decision making process by offering one clear choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2022 - 01/15/2023
On an instant donation page during a year-end survey, we wanted to learn if eliminating friction by reducing the decision making process at the gift array level would help us get more non-donors to say “yes” when we asked for a gift. Rather than making them decide if they want to give $10, $25, $50, $100, $250, or “Other” we developed a treatment that removed all donation amounts other than “$10” and “Other” to make the ask more clear. We ran equal amounts of traffic through each experience and let the experiment run for 3 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the gift array options for non-donors may not achieve an increase in instant donor conversion. The treatment saw a 5.9% decrease in donations for all traffic with a 73.0% statistical level of confidence, and the results cannot be considered valid.

Moreover, the experiment showed that revenue experienced a 217% decrease with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that the treatment's strategy has a negative impact on the organization's revenue, which is not ideal for a nonprofit.

While there was a 741% increase in donations from visitors aged 55-64 with a 99% level of confidence, this finding doesn't provide enough evidence that the treatment is an effective strategy. It's also unclear why this age group responded differently to the treatment.

Therefore, before making online donation changes, it's essential to consider that the gift array options available for donors may impact the organization's revenue negatively. We should test several options cautiously and analyze the data correctly. It's imperative to achieve higher statistical levels of confidence to ensure that the test's results are valid and reliable so that we can use them to make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How offering one option on the gift array impacted gifts from nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that isolating the ask in the gift array to $10 for nondonors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the ask in the copy is more aligned with the options presented in the first step of the form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2023 - 02/17/2023
In a series of testing gift array options, we wanted to better learn how the treatment was impacting conversion rate for non-donors so we continued to locate places to test this concept. One offer was an ask for 400 supporters to chip in $10 to help stop the home equity tax. Since the body copy mentioned specifically a need for $10 donations, we wanted to test if including $10 as the only option on the gift array field would help more non-donors convert to a new donor on this appeal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this A/B experiment conducted by the Canadian Taxpayers Federation are that isolating the ask in the gift array to $10 for non-donors did not achieve an increase in donor conversion. The treatment saw a decrease in donations for all traffic, including a 276% decrease for Mobile visitors and a 753% decrease for Direct visitors. However, due to the statistical level of confidence being below 95%, the results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact on specific visitor segments, as seen in the significant decreases in donations for Mobile and Direct visitors. It may also be beneficial to test different gift array options, including the use of specific donation amounts versus a range of amounts, to see what resonates best with donors. It is important to continue testing and analyzing results to improve conversion rates and optimize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of decisions on a gift array impacted overall giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of decisions on the gift array for our French speaking audience will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there will be less friction in decision making for the visitors when they make their selection.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2022 - 12/31/2022
While testing the impact of gift array choices, we wanted to see if the French-speaking audience saw similar or different results than the English-speaking audience. We created one treatment that kept the choice between one time and monthly giving yet reduced the gift array choice to just $10 or “Other” and we created a second treatment that removed the choice for monthly giving and just asked for one-time gifts of $10 using a single gift array. We split traffic 3 ways between the control and the treatment and ran the experiment for 9 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the gift array options for French-speaking audiences resulted in an increase in donations. However, we cannot confidently validate this result as the statistical level of confidence is below 95%. Moreover, the experiment showed a significant decrease in revenue, which raises concerns about the overall impact of limiting gift array options. In future experimentation, we should consider testing different gift array configurations and analyzing the impact on both donation rates and revenue to make better-informed decisions about gift array options for different audiences. It is also important to ensure that experiments are run long enough to get reliable results and to always perform a proper statistical analysis to avoid flawed conclusions.

experiments.title: How reducing the gift array choices on a video campaign impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the ask in the copy with the ask in the gift array for video viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the gift array matches up with the original ask in the body copy on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2022 - 12/25/2022
In a series of ongoing testing, we wanted to find out if the amount of choices on a gift array was helping or hindering performance. In this case, we were asking for visitors to chip in $10 if they liked the video so it could be shown to 1,000 more people. Thus, the treatment we developed isolated the gift array options to just $10 or “Other” if they wanted to give more or less than choosing the $10 option. We split traffic between the control and treatment and ran the experiment for 16 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the gift array options can potentially increase donor conversion. However, the statistically significant level of confidence of 45.8% is below the recommended level of 95%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid. Therefore, further experimentation and testing is needed to confirm the effectiveness of simplifying gift array options. Future experiments should aim to increase the sample size and statistical power to achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. Additionally, it is important to consider other factors that could impact donor conversion, such as the messaging and design of the donation page.

experiments.title: How presenting a single option in a gift array for video viewers impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because people will have to spent less time considering which choice to make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2022 - 12/25/2022
In a series of testing around gift array options, we wanted to test the impact on donor conversion and revenue by removing the extraneous choices from the gift array menu and offering visitors one option to select to donate $10 or type in the amount of their own choosing in the “Other” field. We split traffic to each version 50/50 and ran the test for two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the number of gift array choices from 6 to 2 for video viewers increased donor conversion by 16.0%. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 62.8%, which means the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making. Future experiments should be designed with larger sample sizes and longer testing periods to ensure statistical significance. Additionally, tests should be conducted on different target audiences to see if these results hold true for other groups. Overall, the experiment suggests that simplifying the donation process may lead to increased donations, but further testing is needed to confirm this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How eliminating gift array choices impacted instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing decision fatigue for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the donor will have to do less work to say yes to what is being asked of them on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2022 - 01/15/2023
In a series of testing around gift array presentation, we wanted to know how results would differ for a post-petition instant donation page with mostly acquisition traffic compared to a survey donation page for primarily email visitors. We set up the same treatments for this segment that we had for the others to measure the overall impact of each version. The first treatment removed the extra gift array options, keeping only $10 and other. The second treatment removed the one time vs. monthly decision from the process and kept only the $10 or “Other” options in tact. Traffic was split equally across all 3 treatments and the experiment ran for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing decision fatigue may increase donor conversion rates for instant donation page visitors, but the results cannot be considered valid due to the low statistical level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment had a negative impact on donations from visitors aged 55-64.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential negative effects of simplifying donation options, particularly for specific age groups. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of gift array options and language to determine the most effective approach for increasing donor conversion rates. It is also important to ensure that experiments have a high enough statistical level of confidence to be considered valid before implementing any changes.

experiments.title: How asking for $20 vs. $10 in a single gift array impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the amount of a single gift array choice for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in revenue because not only will more people will be saying “yes” and they will be saying “yes” to a higher amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2023 - 02/20/2023
Through previous testing, we were identifying some trends that offering donors one $10 gift array was increasing conversion in some instances so we were interested to learn if the increase in conversion was occurring because of the AMOUNT of the ask or purely the SINGULARITY of the ask. So we developed one treatment with the single array of $10 and created a second treatment that kept all elements the same except made the single gift array a $20 option instead. We split traffic equally across the 3 versions and ran the test for 5 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the amount of a single gift array choice in donation pages can result in a slight increase in donations. However, the results are not statistically significant enough to be considered valid. It's also important to note that the treatment resulted in a significant decrease in donations from male visitors and visitors aged 65+. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to test different gift array amounts and language to determine the best combination for increasing donations without negatively impacting certain visitor demographics. It's also important to ensure that experiments are run for a longer duration to obtain statistically significant results.

experiments.title: How eliminating a confirmation message post-survey impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that drawing attention to the donation page copy for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will keep their eyes on the donation page longer rather than leaving immediately upon arrival.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/26/2022
After submitting a survey that led to an instant donation page, we wondered if people were reading the confirmation message “YOU DID IT! Your survey is submitted.” and automatically thinking they had no other action to take on the page and they could click to exit without reading the donation ask immediately following their submission. We developed a treatment that removed the confirmation message and instead led with the headline “One More Thing…” to indicate that their participation in the process was not fully complete. We still mention in the opening paragraph that they filled out the survey and thank them for their time and input. We ran these two versions side by side and measured the impact on donor conversion over a 10 day period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of the A/B experiment showed that the treatment version, which removed the confirmation message and led with the headline "One More Thing", had a 10.7% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control version, with a statistical level of confidence of 40.2%. Although the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, these results suggest that drawing attention to the donation page copy can increase donor conversion rate by keeping their eyes on the page longer.
In future experimentation, we can try to modify the treatment version and test it again to achieve a higher statistical level of confidence. We can also try to identify other factors that might affect donor conversion rate and run further experiments to optimize the donation process. It is essential to keep testing and optimizing the donation page and messaging to increase the number of donations and maximize the impact of the nonprofit's work.

experiments.title: How removing the confirmation language for survey takers impacted gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that continuing the conversation post-survey submission for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because more time will be spent reading the donation page language which makes the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2022 - 12/26/2022
When sending survey takers to an instant donation page upon submission, we wondered if we were signaling in the initial headline “YOU DID IT! Your survey is submitted.” that the user did not need to take any further action and could exit the page without continuing to read the donation page language where we ask them to take the next step and make a gift. We removed the confirmation language in the headline and instead sent visitors to a page that said “One More Thing…” after they submit their survey results so they realize they have to continue to be involved in the process and they’re not necessarily finished yet. We did still recognize early on in the copy that they had filled out the survey and thanked them for their response. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for 11 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that continuing the conversation with visitors after survey submission can result in an increase in donor conversion. The treatment version, which removed the confirmation language in the headline and directed visitors to a page asking them to take one more step, resulted in a 13.4% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 50.7%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.

In future experiments, it is recommended to continue exploring ways to engage with visitors beyond the initial confirmation message to encourage more donations. It is important to ensure that statistical confidence levels are high enough to make valid decisions based on the results of any experiments conducted.

experiments.title: How a simpler gift array impacted gifts on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating decision friction for nondonors after submitting a survey will achieve an increase in $10 donations because the body copy will have synergy with the donation form elements.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2022 - 12/31/2022
When sending non-donors through a survey to an instant donation page, we wondered if removing the one time vs. monthly option and reducing the gift array from multiple options to mirror the ask in the donation page language for 3,000 supporters to donate $10 would improve conversion rate. We split traffic between the two versions and ran the experiment for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that eliminating decision friction for non-donors after submitting a survey can result in an increase in $10 donations. The treatment saw a 15.7% increase in donations for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was only 69.3%, which is below the threshold for valid results. Therefore, the findings cannot be used for future decision making. However, the experiment provides a starting point for future experimentation to optimize the donation process, such as further testing of the language and design of the donation page. The results highlight the importance of carefully evaluating statistical significance and not relying on results below the threshold for valid experimentation.

experiments.title: How breaking down your campaign goal for donors impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that breaking down our fundraising goal into smaller, more relevant asks based upon their last gift amount for donors will achieve increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2022
As the Canadian Taxpayer Federation continued experimenting with breaking their campaign goal down into smaller, more relevant asks, they wanted to experiment with how this type of ask strategy would impact existing donors.
They segmented their donors based upon their “last gift” (range) to also see what the “right ask amount” for this campaign would be.
Those groups were:
Donors whose last gift was around $25.
Donors whose last gift was around $50.
Donors whose last gift was around $100.
Donors whose last gift was around $200.
They then built a treatment like a previous email used for the same campaign that broke down the fundraising goal into one of those four ask amounts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 71.3% level of confidence (which is not fully validated), we observed a decrease in donor conversion rate of -16.9%.
The revenue decrease didn't exactly reflect the decline found in the donor conversion rate — where we observed only a -6.2% decrease in revenue, however that also did not validate (LoC: 60.3%).
When digging into the numbers more closely, we noticed something of interest ... specifically, this tactic actually increased donor conversion rate by +33.7% (albeit with only a 69.2% level of confidence), while we observed a fully valid (LoC: 97.4%) decrease of -39.3% in donor conversion rate when we asked for gifts larger than $100.
Overall, there is some evidence that this organization can lift response rates on asks made to their donors at/below $50, but if they ask for more than $100 in this way, the lack of responses from those segments overall brought the total treatment segment to the aggregate decrease shown above.
Further experimentation may be required, but this points towards using this tactic for smaller ask amounts rather than really large ones for this particular organization.

experiments.title: How a social proof slider showing who just signed a petition impacted petition signature rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does showing who just signed a petition in a notification window to petition page viewers increase petition signature rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2020 - 09/20/2020
As a part of Canadian Taxpayer’s efforts to increase petition signature rates, we decided to experiment with using social proof to create momentum for those considering signing the petition. We decided to show petition page viewers (who had not yet signed the petition) a treatment that displayed a notification in the bottom/left side of their screen showing the first name & province location of the person that just recently signed the petition.
If others also signed the petition while they stayed on the page, new slide-out notifications with the next signer’s information would display to the person on the page, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for nearly a month and collecting 12,000+ total signatures, we could discern no noticeable difference between the two treatments.

What does this mean? It means that the notification window doesn’t do enough to create momentum to improve petition signature rates.

Our overall team did acknowledge that perhaps showing personal details was a “turn off” for some, as much as it might have pushed more to sign, as privacy is really important to some folks in the Canadian Taxpayer’s audience.

This theory is testable, so further experimentation with address data security and privacy is a follow-up experiment we are looking to run next.
